scene.title: Picking a new close-friend after a move to Boston
scene.summary: A young professional who just moved to Boston is weighing two candidates from a Friday co-working meetup.

extras.friendship_context: Two candidates visibly differ in leisure-spend profile; only one matches.

records.scenario_text: Freshly settled in Boston after the move, I met two potential friends at a Friday co-working meetup: Sasha, a lively architect, and Leo, a thoughtful data analyst. Both hinted at monthly hangouts, but their leisure-spending habits differed wildly. For me, building a close friendship through shared activities is worth about $605.7 monthly—whether it's concerts or cozy dinners. Yet every dollar I spend feels like it tugs $2.031 from my budget, almost like a hidden tax on fun, especially with new-city expenses. Sasha gushed about her monthly $766.5 splurges on gallery openings and weekend trips, but she sighed that each leisure dollar stings at $2.737 because she's juggling student loans. Leo shared his $543.1 routine for board-game cafés and hiking gear, noting it only nicks him $1.045 per dollar since his job is stable. Now, I'm stuck: do I sync with Sasha's vibrant but costly rhythm or Leo's lower-key ease? Choosing wrong might strain the friendship before it starts.

teacher_meta.quality_notes: All numbers verbatim embedded; no ratios computed; protagonist choice tension maintained; natural names used; scene ends on hesitation

scene.title: Picking a peer-founder accountability buddy

records.scenario_text: Alex surveyed the buzzing founder dinner, notebook open. As a solo founder, committing to weekly deep-work hours felt crucial but lonely. His own motivation came from valuing each hour at 26.60 for project momentum, though every 60-minute block cost him 1.974 in mental exhaustion. Two candidates stood out. Maya (option A) had shared her stats openly: 'My focus sessions give me 30.96 in creative clarity,' she'd texted earlier, 'but draining 1.514 per hour with my toddler at home.' Ben (option B) chimed in over dessert, sliding his phone across: 'I track mine—32.01 per week in shipped features, though it burns 2.508 hourly with investor calls piling up.' Both wanted accountability, but Alex knew consistency demanded matched rhythms. He tapped his pen, torn between Maya's steady energy and Ben's intense output, wondering whose cadence would truly sync with his own grind.

extras.friendship_context: Two candidates visibly differ in leisure-spend profile; only one matches.

records.scenario_text:
After relocating to Boston, I attended a Friday co-working meetup hoping to forge new connections. Two people stood out: Jake, a laid-back photographer who joked about splurging on concert tickets, and Mia, a bookish data analyst who preferred museum memberships. Both hinted at wanting closer bonds through shared leisure outings—something I desperately needed.

For me, monthly leisure activities deliver genuine joy I'd quantify as 388.0, but every dollar spent carries mental friction—a lingering doubt about affordability that registers as 1.162. Jake mentioned his own leisure happiness peaks around 493.9, though he winced about the 'pinch' per dollar: 'Honestly, each buck stings at 1.435 when rent’s due.' Mia was more lavish, declaring her outings gave her 575.4 in fulfillment, yet stressed, 'The guilt hits harder—1.964 friction per dollar, easy.'

Now I'm torn. Jake’s spontaneity is infectious, but Mia’s tastes align closer to mine. Committing to monthly hangouts means choosing whose rhythm matches mine—where enthusiasm meets similar resistance. Do I invest in Jake’s vibrant but costly vibe, or Mia’s refined yet heavier burden?

teacher_meta.quality_notes: Embedded all numeric values verbatim; surfaced both candidates (Jake, Mia) and protagonist; ended on unresolved tension; avoided ratios/jargon.

scene.title: Picking a tennis hitting partner
scene.summary: A club tennis player deciding which of two mid-level partners to book regular courts with.

extras.friendship_context: Weekly court time is observable; committed partners must match hours.

records.scenario_text: Jamie scanned the club bulletin board, racket bag heavy on their shoulder. Booking a regular tennis partner meant commitment—each weekly hour logged publicly on the court schedule. Mia, a consistent player with sharp volleys, seemed keen. Over coffee last week, she'd mused, 'My satisfaction per hour is maybe 2.664. Effort? Barely 0.646 with my apartment nearby.' Then there was Jake, whose powerful serves masked his cautious approach. He'd texted just yesterday: 'Honestly, my enjoyment sits at 3.560, but parking hassles make each hour cost me 0.652.' Jamie knew their own stakes were higher—this ritual anchored their week, worth 5.179 in pure joy despite the subway trek making every hour feel like 1.222. Both solid options, but the club's booking deadline loomed. Mia's convenience or Jake's enthusiasm? The choice prickled Jamie's palms.

Both seemed promising, but whose sacrifice-and-gain balance would truly sync with her own pace through this marathon program?

scene.title: Picking a regular running partner
scene.summary: A runner new to a Saturday run club deciding who to pair up with for midweek runs.

extras.friendship_context: Running mileage is the visible trait; sustainable friendship needs matching weekly load.

records.scenario_text: The Saturday morning runs with the local club felt invigorating, but Lara knew finding the right midweek partner would make or break her consistency. She'd connected with two runners: Mia, a cheerful nurse training for her first half-marathon, and Jake, a calm architect who valued endurance over speed. Lara reflected on her own running rhythm—each mile brought her 23.32 units of pure satisfaction, though she'd confided in her training journal that every mile demanded 1.567 effort points due to her busy schedule. Mia had gushed over coffee about her marathon prep: 'Honestly, each mile gives me 39.31 joy units!' but later texted about the grind: 'per-mile effort feels like 2.739 lately with night shifts.' Jake shared similar enthusiasm during a cool-down walk: 'My mileage enjoyment hits 40.00 flat,' though he admitted over club messages that 'each mile costs me 2.083 energy-wise after long site visits.' As Tuesday's solo run loomed, Lara stared at her phone, torn between Mia's infectious spirit and Jake's steady pacing—knowing sustaining miles together meant matching invisible thresholds of want and wear.

teacher_meta.quality_notes: Verbatim numeric integration (23.32/1.567; 39.31/2.739; 40.00/2.083); natural names (Lara protagonist, Mia/Jake candidates); ends on active hesitation without resolution; avoids ratios/jargon; contextualizes costs as effort units and benefits as joy/satisfaction.

scene.title: Picking a playdate family in a new neighbourhood
scene.summary: A new-parent family deciding which of two neighbourhood families to deepen ties with.

extras.friendship_context: Observable weekly-outing frequency matches the benefit-cost ratio.

records.scenario_text:
My spouse and I moved to Maple Street last spring, desperate to find our village among the stroller-cluttered sidewalks. Two families caught our eye: the Garcias down the block with their infectious energy, and the quiet Chen family around the corner. We're craving regular Saturday outings together – zoo trips, picnics, maybe even messy pottery classes. For us, the sheer joy these adventures bring feels like a solid 15.81 on our personal 'worth it' scale. But let's be real – prepping snacks, wrangling tantrums, and loading the minivan adds up to a real 2.472 exhaustion tax per outing. 

Jake Garcia gushed that their crew rates the payoff of these weekly escapes at a whopping 21.67! Though his wife Lena did sigh about the logistical headaches scoring a 2.789 on their stress-o-meter. Meanwhile, Emma Chen quietly mentioned her family's enjoyment level lands at 7.106 – lovely but modest – while confessing their effort feels lighter, maybe a 1.163 per trip since their twins are older. 

Now I'm pacing the kitchen, wondering: Do we chase Jake's vibrant enthusiasm despite the grind, or lean into Emma's calmer rhythm? Our Saturday sanity hangs in the balance.

teacher_meta.quality_notes: 179 words. Protagonist benefit (15.81) and cost (2.472) embedded organically. Candidate A (Jake/Lena: benefit 21.67, cost 2.789) and Candidate B (Emma: benefit 7.106, cost 1.163) surfaced with names and exact values. Ended with tension between choices. Avoided jargon/ratios.
